title: Panels
intro: A panel is collaborative chrome: it slides in beside the content without blocking it. No shim, no overlay — the page stays live while the panel is open. If the user must finish the panel before touching the page, the surface is a Drawer, not a panel.

items.rule: A panel never blocks the page — no shim, no overlay, no dimming.
items.why: The point of a panel is side-by-side work. If the page must freeze while the surface is open, the surface is a Drawer.

items.rule: Panels push content aside via flex; they never float above it.
items.why: A floating overlay hides the very data the panel exists to support. Pushing keeps both surfaces fully visible and interactive.

items.rule: Budget chrome before you build — cumulative non-grid chrome on a grid page stays at or under 320px.
items.why: Panels ship tight from the first pass; chrome is budgeted, not accumulated. Headers, toolbars, and panel padding all spend from the same budget, and the grid must keep the viewport.

items.rule: Inline panels are 320–400px fixed-width; the main pane takes flex 1 with min-width 0.
items.why: Fixed width keeps panel content stable while the main pane absorbs the resize. min-width 0 lets the flex pane actually shrink instead of overflowing under the panel.

items.rule: Every panel owns its scroll — pinned header, independently scrolling body.
items.why: Tying panel scroll to the page scrolls the user's working context away mid-task.

items.rule: An inline panel's header is title left, close (✕) right, 1px border below — nothing else. Split-panel list headers drop the close: there is no open/close state to dismiss.
items.why: A constant header anatomy makes every panel instantly identifiable — and dismissible when dismissal exists.

items.rule: Split-panel lists are 280–320px fixed on the left; detail takes flex 1 on the right.
items.why: List labels truncate predictably at a fixed width; detail content is what benefits from every extra pixel.

items.rule: Panels close from their own header or their launch control — never from a click on the main content.
items.why: Click-outside-to-close belongs to overlays. Panels exist precisely so users can click the content while they work.

blocks.title: Panel vs Drawer
blocks.caption: The dividing line: a panel keeps the content area interactive with no shim; a drawer deactivates it behind an overlay. Panels serve filters, settings, and detail previews — drawers serve creation forms and multi-step workflows.

blocks.title: Inline panel — right side
blocks.caption: The canonical inline panel: main content shrinks via flex and stays fully active; the panel sits at fixed width with a border-left, pinned title + close header, and its own scrolling body.

blocks.title: Split panel — list + detail
blocks.caption: Persistent two-pane anatomy: fixed-width list pane on the left with selection state (tinted row + 3px accent bar), flex-1 detail pane on the right, 1px border between. Both panes scroll independently.

blocks.title: Do / Don't — push, never overlay
blocks.caption: Do: panel shares the flex row and the content stays active. Don't: a drawer-style overlay that dims or blocks the page — if the content is blocked, the surface should be a Drawer.

blocks.title: Panel types — and the drawer boundary
blocks.intro: Two panel shapes cover every legitimate use. Anything past them is a Drawer.

rows.name: Inline panel
rows.when: Transient tool surface the user opens and closes — filters, settings, detail preview that follows selection. 320–400px fixed, border-left, mounted inside the page's flex row.

rows.name: Split panel
rows.when: Persistent two-pane list + detail anatomy — profile managers, manage tabs. Left list 280–320px fixed, right detail flex 1; both always present, no open/close state.

rows.name: Drawer — not a panel
rows.when: The task must block the page: creation forms, multi-step workflows, formula editors. Shim overlay, explicit close, commit/cancel contract. See the Right Drawers entry.

blocks.title: Panel sizing & spacing
blocks.intro: Fixed dimensions are decided once per surface and never accumulated. The chrome budget is the binding constraint.

rows.use: Fixed width of an inline push panel — pick once based on densest content, then hold it
Fixed left pane of a split panel; detail pane takes flex 1
Cumulative non-grid chrome on grid pages: page header + toolbars + footers + panel padding stack, all-in
Panel header vertical padding; gap between fields in the panel body
Panel header horizontal padding; panel body padding
Panel edge: border-left on inline panels, divider between split panes, header border-bottom
Panel background when it should read as secondary to the main content; list-pane headers

blocks.title: Canonical inline panel
blocks.note: Visibility is a plain conditional render — no shim component, no portal. Layout props go through the Horizontal/Vertical shorthands (`flex="1"`, `width={360}`, `height="100%"`, `gap={12}`, `scroll`, `justifyContent`), never through `style`; `style` carries only borders, padding, and the `minWidth: 0` shrink fix.

items.do: Push the content aside — panel and page share one flex row.
items.dont: Float the panel over the page with a shadow or dimmed backdrop.
items.why: Dimmed or blocked content means the surface is a Drawer. Mislabeled panels train users that the page is frozen when it is not.

items.do: Ship the panel at its final, tight dimensions in the first pass.
items.dont: Stack header, toolbar, padding, and panel chrome incrementally until the grid loses the viewport.
items.why: Chrome is budgeted, not accumulated — cumulative non-grid chrome stays at or under 320px on grid pages.

items.do: Give the panel a pinned header and an independently scrolling body.
items.dont: Let one scrollbar move the panel and the main content together.
items.why: Side-by-side work breaks the moment scrolling one surface scrolls the other away.

items.do: Close from the ✕ in the panel header or by toggling the launch control.
items.dont: Dismiss the panel when the user clicks the main content.
items.why: Clicking the content is the panel's core use case, not a dismissal gesture.

items.title: Chrome budget: 320px, all-in
items.detail: Panels ship tight from the first pass — chrome is budgeted, not accumulated. On grid pages, cumulative non-grid chrome (page header + toolbars + footers + the panel's own padding stack) must stay at or under 320px or the grid no longer fits the viewport. Audit the running total before adding a panel; if the panel pushes past the budget, trim other chrome — do not ship and tighten later.

items.title: min-width: 0 on the flex pane
items.detail: Flex children refuse to shrink below their content's intrinsic width. Without `minWidth: 0` on the main pane, opening the panel makes a grid overflow underneath it instead of resizing. Pair with a grid that re-measures on container resize.

items.title: Graduating to a Drawer? Use the v5 prop names
items.detail: When a surface outgrows panel rules and becomes a Drawer, use the antd v5 names: `open` (not `visible`), `destroyOnHidden` (not `destroyOnClose`), and `afterOpenChange` (not `afterVisibleChange`). Drawer has no `onOpenChange` — wire dismissal through `onClose`.

blocks.title: Choosing panel vs drawer
blocks.body:
The decision is interaction, not size. Ask one question: does the user need to see and touch the main content while the surface is open? Yes — panel: filters that live-update a grid, detail previews that follow row selection, settings that apply immediately. No — drawer: creation forms, multi-step workflows, formula editors, anything with a commit/cancel contract.

Inline panels are for transient tools with open/close state; split panels are for permanent list + detail anatomy and carry no open/close state at all. When a "panel" starts wanting a footer with Save and Cancel, it has already become a drawer — move it to the Right Drawers pattern rather than bolting a commit contract onto a non-blocking surface.
